posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked: Do those of you experienced with old Bordeaux tend to treat them like Burgundy? I was thinking that the delicacy of these wines might warrant that. By “treat like Burgundy”, I mean drinking them out of big Burgundy stems. Or do you stick to Bordeaux stems?

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked: Not my forte, but we use the Grassl Cru for Borgeaux as well as Burgundy. And I prefer the Zalto Bordeaux stem to the Zalto Burg stem for both Bordeaux and Pinot Noir(Burgundy or otherwise).

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: Old Bordeaux isn’t especially delicate. But why would delicate wines require big glasses?

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
the zalto bordeaux glass looks like a classic burgundy glass and the zalto burgundy glass looks like a very drunk bordeaux glass.

i doubt this information is useful, and for that i apologize.

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
Old Bordeaux isn’t especially delicate. But why would delicate wines require big glasses?

Oh I don’t know. Just thinking that the more delicate aromas might develop more nicely in a bigger bowl. But that is 100% conjecture, which is why I turned to the forum here.

The real motivation behind my question is that I have some nice older Bordeaux that I’d like to open soon-ish. I am very much looking forward to trying them. I’ve got some really nice Burgundy stems, but only so-so Bordeaux stems. Wondering which ones would best to serve the old Bordeaux. Maybe the solution is to buy nice Bordeaux stems!

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked: Not sure what it is you consider old, but I would think that the more “delicate” a wine is, the more controlled it needs to be to exposure to air. Probably why a lot of old Burgs aren’t decanted.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Just as Bordeaux needs to go into straight tall bottles, a Bordeaux stem should be used.
Call me a traditionalist.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Old Bordeaux isn’t especially delicate. But why would delicate wines require big glasses?

Require is a particularly strong word. No wine really requires a glass at all(I know this to be true from my mispent youth).

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Just as Bordeaux needs to go into straight tall bottles, a Bordeaux stem should be used.
Call me a traditionalist.

Nothing wrong with that. And in multiple tastings of glassware, good Bordeaux stems do not fare well at all with Pinot Noir. Zalto excepted for the excellent reason Yaacov noted.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Do those of you experienced with old Bordeaux tend to treat them like Burgundy? I was thinking that the delicacy of these wines might warrant that. By “treat like Burgundy”, I mean drinking them out of big Burgundy stems. Or do you stick to Bordeaux stems?

In terms of glassware, wider bowls bring out the pyrazine aromas inherent to the Cabernet-family varieties, so I find more classically tulip-shaped Bordeaux glasses work better.

It depends how you define “old”, but I have found, repeatedly, that wines from good vintages back to at least the 1950s benefit from aeration in a decanter. Wine that can seem lean, or even a little tired, “pop” and display much more fruit and mid-palate volume after decanting. I think people are often too afraid to try it, and thus never see what some of these wines have to offer. Of course, all that’s negated if you stir up all the sediment, so proceed carefully.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: I used the Riedel Vinum Bordeaux glass for Bordeaux and the Riedel Vinum Burgundy glass for Burgundy.

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked:
Do those of you experienced with old Bordeaux tend to treat them like Burgundy? I was thinking that the delicacy of these wines might warrant that. By “treat like Burgundy”, I mean drinking them out of big Burgundy stems. Or do you stick to Bordeaux stems?

In terms of glassware, wider bowls bring out the pyrazine aromas inherent to the Cabernet-family varieties, so I find more classically tulip-shaped Bordeaux glasses work better.

It depends how you define “old”, but I have found, repeatedly, that wines from good vintages back to at least the 1950s benefit from aeration in a decanter. Wine that can seem lean, or even a little tired, “pop” and display much more fruit and mid-palate volume after decanting. I think people are often too afraid to try it, and thus never see what some of these wines have to offer. Of course, all that’s negated if you stir up all the sediment, so proceed carefully.

I defer to both your knowledge and experience, but it seems to me decanting a 40-70 year old Burgundy to be quite a craps shoot. I’d be inclined to follow it in the glass. Would make an interesting poll.

posts.cooked:

The thread is about Bordeaux, not Burgundy, but I assume that’s just a typo on your part?

The results of the poll would only be interesting if people who have never done it, and thus have no salient experience, were excluded from responding.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked: Noah–tell us how old your Bordeaux is. My guess is you’ll want to decant it for an hour or two as described above, and use a standard bordeaux stem.

posts.name: John Telford
posts.cooked: I drink an abundance of Bordeaux. No I would not drink out of a “burgundy” glass. And, in my humble opinion, it is not delicate or fragile. Clearly, there are anomalies but as a rule NO.

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked:

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I defer to both your knowledge and experience, but it seems to me decanting a 40-70 year old Burgundy to be quite a craps shoot. I’d be inclined to follow it in the glass. Would make an interesting poll.

The thread is about Bordeaux, not Burgundy, but I assume that’s just a typo on your part?

The results of the poll would only be interesting if people who have never done it, and thus have no salient experience, were excluded from responding.

The poll would not be whether you are correct or not, but would they take the chance.

I suspect most wouldn’t/don’t.

I used to be quite risk averse about decanting myself, and it was only in playing around with multiple bottles of the same wine that I learnt otherwise. One wine I opened a lot of, in bottles and magnums, was the 1970 Pape Clément. A little thin and even metallic when popped and poured, we figured out that, for a magnum, after about five hours decant, it became, if not quite a fruit bomb, at the very least a fleshy, dramatic, lush wine. It was in its prime after a decant, but on opening one could easily have thought it a bit long in the tooth. I think wines like this are the biggest casualties of not decanting, as they present a totally different and much less interesting personality without aeration. A wine such as 1961 Haut-Brion or 1982 Lafleur certainly gains a lot with decanting, too, but is at least obviously great whether popped-and-poured or decanted, so you’re not going to miss its quality either way.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Not decanting old Bordeaux off sediment and then letting it open are both mistakes in my experience. 1970 Pape Clement, per WK above and also one of my favorite birth year wines still, is one of innumerable examples why. They are not delicate wines unless the bottle is already a goner. Of course, you can sample along the way.

Drink your Bordeaux in a Bordeaux glass. You won’t regret it. Or better for your education, try side by side with a Burg bowl. You will experience the difference. No reason to deprive yourself of the side by side experience by locking yourself into one path or the other in advance.

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked:
Not decanting old Bordeaux off sediment and then letting it open are both mistakes in my experience. 1970 Pape Clement, per WK above and also one of my favorite birth year wines still, is one of innumerable examples why. They are not delicate wines unless the bottle is already a goner. Of course, you can sample along the way.

Drink your Bordeaux in a Bordeaux glass. You won’t regret it. Or better for your education, try side by side with a Burg bowl. You will experience the difference. No reason to deprive yourself of the side by side experience by locking yourself into one path or the other in advance.

The bolded sentence captures my experience exactly. I can’t recall any sound older red wines I’ve opened in the past 5 years that have not benefitted from a gentle decant, including a number of Bordeaux and Barolos that were 35 or more years old.

As for glasses, I’ve tried most of them, including all of the Zaltos and Grassls, and I keep finding that I prefer Gabriel Glas Golds with everything, particularly with a tasting pour. However, I discovered tonight that I like the Grassl 1855 for cognac.

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
Noah–tell us how old your Bordeaux is. My guess is you’ll want to decant it for an hour or two as described above, and use a standard bordeaux stem.

I am most concerned about a 1964 Chateau Latour. I got this bottle at a ridiculously discount because of the low fill level- there’s a decent chance it’s a bottle of vinegar. The shop owner, who I think really knows his stuff, said that if the wine is intact it will be very fragile, should not be decanted, and he even recommended to put a stopper into the bottle between pours in order to halt the ingress of oxygen. So this is the wine I was originally referring to when I was wondering about the Burgundy stems.

But I also have a few bottles from the earlier to mid 80s. I was curious about those as well.

Thanks for all the suggestions!

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
64 Latour is still a very vigorous wine unless the bottle is already damaged / cooked. Low fill suggests it’s not in great shape. But if it turns out to be just fine and you want this puppy to have the chance to shine, you would be wrong not to stand it for a couple weeks, decant off sediment, and give it plenty of air.

And put it in a Bordeaux glass. No question.

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
Noah–tell us how old your Bordeaux is. My guess is you’ll want to decant it for an hour or two as described above, and use a standard bordeaux stem.

I am most concerned about a 1964 Chateau Latour. I got this bottle at a ridiculously discount because of the low fill level- there’s a decent chance it’s a bottle of vinegar. The shop owner, who I think really knows his stuff, said that if the wine is intact it will be very fragile, should not be decanted, and he even recommended to put a stopper into the bottle between pours in order to halt the ingress of oxygen. So this is the wine I was originally referring to when I was wondering about the Burgundy stems.

But I also have a few bottles from the earlier to mid 80s. I was curious about those as well.

Thanks for all the suggestions!

Some years ago I bought a 1958 Latour at auction, mid to low shoulder and no idea on provenance. Drank on a combined 60th three years ago. 1958 is generally regarded at best as an average vintage. Our hopes were low. The bottle was outstanding. We decanted about 2 hours before serving. Latour is well known for rising about the vintage and producing robust and long lived wines even in relatively “poor” vintages.

I would say you are likely to be pleasantly surprised and also definitely follow Jayson’s advice…

Cheers Brodie

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked: And your wines from the early to mid eighties are not fragile oldsters, they are just mature Bordeaux hopefully ready to drink. those certainly would benefit from a decant 1-2 hours, and a standard glass.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
In terms of glassware, wider bowls bring out the pyrazine aromas inherent to the Cabernet-family varieties, so I find more classically tulip-shaped Bordeaux glasses work better.

If I remember correctly, you mentioned in another discussion some time ago, that you go with the Sensory for almost all wines these days. My side by side showed that the Sensory dramatically outperforms most other glasses (Gabriel, Grassl; narrowly the Zaltos) for almost all young and older wines, but I did not do tests on 50+ year old wines. Does your reply mean that for old Bdxs you prefer the Bordeaux glass (from Zalto I suspect)?

posts.name: Christopher Foley
posts.cooked:
I double decanted the '61 Latour I opened last March. I did this in the morning and, at dinner the wine was outstanding.
Double decant 8 or so hours ahead is my normal treatment of mature Bordeaux. The wine gains enough air from moving twice, then resting, and it opens up quickly in the glass. And I like serving from the bottle, without fear of sediment.
I took a bottle of 1986 Gruaud Larose to Maine for dinner after double decanting in the morning in Connecticut. That was a 95 pointer.
At home I use Grassl 1855s for wine like that.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
In terms of glassware, wider bowls bring out the pyrazine aromas inherent to the Cabernet-family varieties, so I find more classically tulip-shaped Bordeaux glasses work better.

If I remember correctly, you mentioned in another discussion some time ago, that you go with the Sensory for almost all wines these days. My side by side showed that the Sensory dramatically outperforms most other glasses (Gabriel, Grassl; narrowly the Zaltos) for almost all young and older wines, but I did not do tests on 50+ year old wines. Does your reply mean that for old Bdxs you prefer the Bordeaux glass (from Zalto I suspect)?

I’m a big fan and early adopter of the Sensory glasses, but I only really use then for Burgundy reds and whites/PN & Chardonnay from elsewhere, Rhône (and not all), and Piemonte. For me, I pretty much simply replaced the Zalto Burgundy glass, always my least favorite from them, with the Sensory, and kept Zalto white wine, Universal and BDX in rotation. For old Bordeaux I might go for any of those three, depending on how old: the 1921 Margaux we drank last month we had from the white wine glasses, in fact, because it was so delicate it wasn’t clear a big glass would flatter it.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked: Many thanks, that’s really helpful

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked:
I don’t drink a lot of older Burgundy, so I’ll certainly punt on how to treat it. In fact, most of the times I’ve had older Burgundy, it’s been served by somebody else, so it wasn’t even up to me…

As for Bordeaux, I think decanting is basically always beneficial, but I’ll still usually decant it AND follow the trajectory of the wine over time … from the little pour right out of the bottle to check that it’s sound until the last glass out of the decanter, perhaps hours later. That journey scratches my hobbyist itch, so I get to enjoy and learn. OTOH, if I were serving it as part of a paced meal to guests (and finishing the entire bottle within a span of a single course of food split between several people), I’d want the wine to show it’s best while it’s being consumed. Roughly an hour of air seems to be a safe time for most BDX, but the nice thing about BDX is that - generally speaking - there’s tons of it to go around, so there should be lots of CT notes.

posts.name: Lawrence Leichtman
posts.cooked: I use the Reidel Vinum series for both Bordeaux and Burgundy. Really big bowls seem to emphasize off aromas to me.

fancy_title: Do you treat old Bordeaux like Burgundy?
title: Do you treat old Bordeaux like Burgundy?
